target.name: Diraq
target.founder: Prof. Andrew Dzurak (CEO, Scientia Professor UNSW Sydney, pioneer of silicon spin-qubit technology)

methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $42M total raised proxy.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Quantum Computing
Public-co frontier-tech comp in Quantum Computing
Public-co frontier-tech comp in Quantum Computing

data.narrative:
Diraq is a Series A quantum computing company founded by Prof. Andrew Dzurak, a Scientia Professor at UNSW Sydney and pioneer of silicon spin-qubit technology. The company has raised $42M to date. The relevant comp universe consists of three public quantum computing firms (IONQ, RGTI, QBTS); no private quantum peers with comparable financing data are available.

Given the limited comp universe and Diraq's early-stage positioning, stage-progression methodology is the applicable triangulation approach. Using total raised ($42M) as a valuation proxy and applying a standard Series A-to-next-stage step-up multiplier of 2.0x yields an implied valuation of $84M. This method assumes market precedent for capital efficiency and step-up magnitude at this stage, though the single-method foundation introduces methodological risk.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $71M (low) to $97M (high), with a median of $84M. Key uncertainties include the absence of private-market comparables in the quantum sector, which limits cross-validation of the step-up multiplier, and the nascent, capital-intensive nature of quantum hardware development, which may not follow standard venture stage progression patterns. Additional data on burn rate, technical milestones, and competitive positioning would strengthen conviction in this range.
